title: Reactivity of surface groups attached on a plasma treated poly(propylene) film. Application to a new concept of a chelating membrane
abstract:
212
Reactivity of surface groups attached on a plasma
treated poly(propylene) film. Application to a new
concept of a chelating membrane
Fabienne Poncin-Epaillard*, Nicolas Me´dard, Jean-Claude Soutif
Unite´ Mixte de Recherche Chimie et Physique des Mate´riaux Polyme`res, (UMR n8 6515), Universite´ du Maine,
avenue O. Messiaen, 72000 Le Mans, France
epaillard@aviion.univ-lemans.fr
(Received: March 15, 1999; revised: May 7, 1999)
Introduction
The membrane technique as an industrial process of
separation and purification has been recently emphasized.
With the aims of a better selectivity and a higher flow, the
membrane technology is nowadays developed in the
direction of membranes with chelating or separating
properties in addition to filtration properties where the
surface phenomena are not negligible. This kind of mem-
brane can be prepared in different ways from an already
existing membrane.
The most popular way is the post-grafting of a vinyl
monomer induced by an electron beam, followed by a che-
mical modification leading to the chelating groups. Most of
the work done on the subject is focused on the metal recov-
ery from water and especially from seawater. For example,
the dissolved uranium concentration in the oceans is esti-
mated to 4.5 109 tons, that is to say one thousand time higher
than the resources worldwide. However, the dissolved ura-
nium concentration in seawater remains low: 3.3 mg/m3.
Saito et al.1) described the synthesis of a membrane bearing
amidoxime groups for chelating uranium. Such a method –
post-grafting of acrylonitrile monomer and then reaction of
the grafted membrane with hydroxylamine – is quite simple
and enables the development of a membrane possessing
good properties in terms of adsorption kinetics, high selec-
tivity towards uranium and long life time (without any
aging after 10cyclesof process).
The iminodiacetic acid group is also selected for che-
lating radioactive cobalt dissolved in cooling water of
nuclear power stations2). In this case, glycidyl methacryl-
ate was grafted onto a polyethylene membrane, then the
epoxy group was opened with iminodiacetic acid. This
membrane is described as chemically stable and as pos-
sessing a high affinity to metal ions (2 mol/kg).
A separation membrane based on a microporous poly-
(propylene) is described for dry secondary lithium bat-
teries3). Its elaboration consists in the pre-irradiation of
poly(propylene) film, then in the grafting of acrylic acid
by dipping the pre-irradiated membrane in a monomer
solution containing also diethylene glycol dimethacrylate
as a cross-linking reagent. It allows the development of a
new generation of secondary lithium batteries with an
important exchange capacity (135 Ah/kg for a 3.2 V volt-
age) and a high reversibility (X95% after 60 cycles).
Macromol. Chem. Phys. 201, No. 2
i WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69451 Weinheim 2000
1022-1352/2000/0201–0212$17.50+.50/0
The goal of the present work is the synthesis of a new che-
lating membrane from a trivial material, such as poly(pro-
pylene), through the fixation of the chelating groups after
a cold plasma irradiation.
The cold plasma treatment compared to the electron beam
irradiation is less degrading since only a few layers are
affected. It leads to a surface bearing two types of reactive
species: radicals able to initiate a post-grafting reaction of
any kind of vinyl monomer and new surface functional
groups – depending on the chemical nature of the cold
plasma – able to react with a chelating molecule. So the
modulation of the plasma conditions and the choice of the
attached chelating groups onto the material can control the
chelating capacity of such irradiated material. The nitro-
gen plasma modification of poly(propylene) (PP) leads to
a weakly cross-linked and slightly degraded surface bear-
ing alkyl radicals and amino groups in various densities
depending on plasma conditions. After the plasma irradia-
tion, N-acryloylglycine (NAG) and diethylenetriamine-
pentaacetic acid (DTPA) possessing specific chelating
properties are grafted successfully onto the PP surface in
different ways. All the grafted films present an important
selectivity towards silver ions and consequently towards
soft metallic ions, too. The ion capacity of NAG grafted
films remains higher than with DTPA whatever the density
of amino groups attached on the surface is.
Macromol. Chem. Phys. 201, 212–219 (2000)
 Reactivity of surface groups attached on a plasma treated poly(propylene) film ...
213
The goal of the present work is the synthesis of a new
chelating membrane from a common material such as
poly(propylene) and the fixation of the chelating groups
through a cold plasma irradiation.
The cold plasma treatment compared to the electron
beam irradiation is less degrading since only few layers
are affected and it provides a surface bearing two types of
reactive species: radicals able to initiate a post-grafting
reaction of any kind of vinyl monomer4, 5) and new sur-
face functional groups depending on the chemical nature
of the cold plasma6) able to react with a chelating mole-
cule. So, the chelating capacity of such irradiated material
can be controlled by the modulation of the plasma condi-
tions and by the type of attached chelating groups on the
material.
Recently, Michel et al.7) succeeded in introducing pyri-
dine ligands with coordinating properties by modifying
the surface of a polyacrylonitrile membrane using He
plasma treatment and post-grafting reaction of a pyridine
derivative. Such a material may have applications in the
facilitated transport of CO2 or halogen ions.
The nitrogen plasma modification of poly(propylene)
(PP) fully described elsewhere6) provides a weakly cross-
linked and less degraded surface bearing alkyl radicals
and amino groups in various densities depending on
plasma conditions. The formation of amino groups comes
from an in situ reaction with hydrogen fragment from the
polymer surface degradation and nitrogen plasma. After
the plasma irradiation, the N-acryloylglycine (NAG) and
diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA) having speci-
fic chelating properties8) are grafted onto the PP surface
by different routes, the chelating properties of the
obtained films are tested.
Experimental part
Materials
Poly(propylene)
Exempt of any kind of additives, poly(propylene) supplied
by “Institut Textile de France (ITF)” and obtained by extru-
sion, was synthesized with a Ziegler-Natta catalyst in a het-
erogeneous phase. It is an isotactic semi-crystalline polymer
(melting point: 1608 C) with a thickness of 100 lm. The
crystalline phase corresponds to a smectic state in a percen-
tage between 52–55% (determined respectively by X-ray
scattering and FTIR spectroscopy) and 62% (determined
with differential scanning calorimetry). Another isotactic PP
film (Goodfellow) with 12 lm thickness is also used for the
N2/H2 plasma treatment.
Surface area is calculated from the weight, the density
(given by the supplier) and the thickness of each sample.
The purity of gases, purchased from Air Liquide is as fol-
lows:
nitrogen (grade U), purity A99.998%,
hydrogen (grade U), purity A99.998%,
N-acryloylglycine (NAG) synthesis9, 10)
25 ml
of
an
aqueous
solution
of
glycine
(10.5 g,
140 mmol), sodium hydroxide (4.4 g, 110 mmol) and 2,6-di-
tert-butyl-p-cresol (0.02 g) were cooled at –188C, then aque-
ous sodium hydroxide (4.0 g, 100 mmol in 25 ml) and acryl-
oyl chloride (7.93 g, 90 mmol) were added simultaneously
and slowly. The solution was stirred at room temperature
during one hour, and then its pH was adjusted to 2 with con-
centrated hydrochloric acid. After filtration, the water was
evaporated, the filtrate dried, and extracted with boiling
ethyl acetate (100 ml). After recrystallisation, the reaction
yield reached 40–50%. The reaction product was character-
ized by NMR and IR:
1H NMR: d = 4.0 (CH21COOH), 5.8 (CH22CH), 6.4
(CH22CH), 8.6 (NH), 12.8 (COOH).
IR: 989 (dCH2, CH22CH), 1229 (mC1O, HO1C2O),
1539 (dNH), 1602 (mC2C), 1642 (mC2O, O2C1NH1),
1714 (mC2O, HO1C2O), 2800–3000 (mCH2), 3348 cm–1
(mNH).
DTPA anhydride synthesis11)
A solution of 3.93 g (10 mmol) of DPTA, 3.78 ml (40 mmol)
of acetic anhydride and 4.84 ml (60 mmol) of pyridine was
stirred under reflux during one hour. Then the precipitate
was filtered, washed with acetic anhydride and anhydrous
ether and dried. The yield was 95%.

NAG grafting
DTPA fixation

Plasma equipment and plasma treatment
The equipment used here is a microwave plasma apparatus
consisted of three parts. The plasma excitation is provided
by a microwave generator (433 MHz) with a variable power
(0–250 W), which is coupled to a resonant cavity. The inci-
dent power (Pi) and the reflected power (Pr) are measured
with a power-meter (Hewlett-Packard, 435B). The impe-
dance is adjusted until the reflected power is very low
(Pr a 10–2 W). The glow is generated at the top of the reactor.
The pumping system is composed of primary (CIT Alcatel
2012) and oil diffusion (CIT Alcatel Crystal) pumps. A MKS
mass flow-meter type 1259B controls the gas flow (D in
sccm). The pressure (p in bar) is measured with Penning and
Pirani gauges. A quartz cylinder of 500 mm length and
76 mm diameter serves as reactor. The reactor is placed on a
chamber used for the sample introduction. The substrate can
be moved in or out of the plasma volume. The distance
between the bottom of the excitator and the sample d (in
Before each run, the system was kept at 10–6 mbar (second-
ary vacuum) for at least 2 h. The treatment procedure was as
follows: after setting up the film on the holder, the reactor
pressure was reduced below 1 N 10–1 mbar (primary vacuum)
and the gas was introduced 5 min before the treatment. Then
the treatment is achieved. Optical emission spectroscopy and
mass spectrometry control the purity of the plasma.
After the plasma treatment, the film was immediately dipped
into a solution of NAG in water in different concentrations
(from 0 to 18 wt.-%). The sample and the reaction tube were
kept in nitrogen atmosphere to minimize oxidation. The graft-
ing reaction is performed during various times (from 0 to
90 h) at 908C with Mohr’s salt as homo-polymerization inhi-
bitor (0.1 wt.-%), then the grafted sample was extracted in a
Soxhlet apparatus with boiling water during 48 h and dried
under vacuum (without degradation of the grafted layer).
After the plasma treatment, the film was immediately dipped
into a solution of DTPA anhydride in DMSO in different
concentrations (from 0 to 25 wt.-%). The sample and the
reaction tube were kept in nitrogen atmosphere to minimize
oxidation. The grafting reaction was performed during 72 h
at room temperature, and then the grafted sample was
extracted in a Soxhlet apparatus with boiling water during
Analyses were performed on an apparatus ESCA LHS 12
(Leybold) at the “Institut des Mate´riaux de Nantes” (CNRS).
The incident X-ray corresponds to the Ka ray of Magnesium
at 1.2536 keV with an incident angle of 608 (Y10 nm
depth). Calibration due to the charge effect was made on the
C1s peak (C1C, C1H) at 284.6 eV.
IRRAS analysis was performed on a spectrometer Bruker
n8IFS 66 with a specular reflection cell (H = 708, resolution:
2 cm–1, 200 scans). The area measurement was based on a
straight baseline connecting the Y-values of the left and right
Spectroscopic titrations are run on an UV-visible spectro-
meter Varian DMS 100 using a quartz cell (1 cm length
Surface amino groups titration
Surface amines were titrated by using Ponceau 2R [3-
hydroxy-4-(2,4-methylphenylazo)-2,7-naphthalenedisulfonic
acid, disodium salt] acidified with 0.1 N HCl solution
Immediately after treatment and without exposure to air,
the PP sample was dipped into a 10 ml solution of the
mol N l–1). After degassing, the solution was heated up to
708C for 12 h while stirring. Then a back titration of the
solution was made on the UV spectrometer. The temperature
effect can be negligible because these titrations were run
below the a phase transition of PP (808); treated surfaces
were not altered by exposure to water and weak acidic solu-
tions. A blank titration was also run. The amino group con-
centration on the virgin poly(propylene) was found to be
neglected (practically zero). Purity, stability and activity of
each reagent solution were checked before each titration.
Surface carboxylic groups titration
The grafted films were dipped into an ethanol solution of
thionine acetate (Fluka) (1 N 10–5 mol N l–1). The reaction solu-
tion was stirred at room temperature during 10 h. Then the
film was removed and the solution was titrated by measuring
its UV adsorption and comparing with a blank solution. The
difference between the two adsorptions was proportional to
the carboxylic acid groups attached onto the film.
The pH-metric and conductimetric titrations were run on a
Metrohm Titro-processor 686 apparatus. The addition of the
reagent is controlled with a conductimeter Consort K220
using platinum electrodes. The titration is run in aqueous
medium (50 ml) at room temperature with a fresh sodium
Silver nitrate (AgNO3), cupric nitrate hydrate (Cu(NO3)2 N
6 H2O) and anhydrous ferric chloride (FeCl3) were used
without any purification. Complexometric analyses were run
Reactivity of surface groups attached on a plasma treated poly(propylene) film ...
on an ICP 6500 (Perkin-Elmer) apparatus or on an absorption
spectrometer (Solun AM 929). The grafted film is dipped
into the metal solution at room temperature during 48 h.
After removing and washing the film, the remained solution
is titrated after calibration with standard solutions.
Two nitrogen plasma conditions were chosen for the film
treatment: a corresponds to the highest concentration of
the amino groups (2 NH2/nm2 for 40 radicals/nm2) for
DTPA anhydride reaction and b to a maximum concentra-
tion of radicals (60 radicals/nm2 and 0.6 NH2/nm2)6) for
t = 2 min, d = 10 cm, D = 20 sccm,
b: Pi = 100 W, t = 5 min, d = 4 cm,
The FTIR-ATR spectrum of a PP film treated under condi-
tions a and grafted with NAG presents characteristic bands
of polyNAG (mC2O of O2C1OH groups at 1714 cm–1,
mC2O of O2C1NH groups at 1642 cm–1, dNH of
O2C1NH groups at 1539 cm–1, mC1O of O2C1OH
groups at 1229 cm–1, dOH of O2C1OH groups at 1104
The XPS spectra of the treated and grafted samples
present C1s, O1s and N1s peaks. Their relative percentages
(in atom %), given in Tab. 1, depend on the experimental
conditions. The observed oxygen on the virgin sample
spectrum corresponds to quenched oxygen during the
film process. The carbon and oxygen content raise after
grafting the treated surface, so the grafting seems to be
effective. Comparing the relative intensity of each peak
with the theoretical stoichiometry of polyNAG (%C = 56,
%O = 33, %N = 11), the increase of the C1s peak intensity
is more important than that of the oxygen or nitrogen
peaks showing that the analyzed thickness is more impor-
tant than the grafted layer. However, the relative intensi-
ties for the sample treated under conditions a and grafted
are close to the theoretical values of polyNAG. So, it
seems that the grafting reaction is enhanced when the PP
film is treated under such conditions, which favor amino
groups attachment, as it has been already observed with
The grafting yield determined as the area of the car-
boxylic group vibration band between 1810–1580 cm–1
on the FTIR spectrum depends on the monomer concen-
tration in the grafting solution (Fig. 1). Increasing the
monomer concentration increases the grafting yield until
a critical concentration of 10% of monomer for the b con-
ditions and 12% for a conditions.
The grafting yield is also determined by an acid-base
titration5, 12) of the formed carboxylic groups with 5 N 10–4
N aqueous sodium hydroxide solution run with a pH-
meter and conductimeter (Fig. 2). The same conclusions
as those with FTIR-ATR spectroscopy can be drawn: the
highest amido acetic group density (1140/nm2) is reached
XPS analysis of PP films treated with nitrogen plasma
(conditions a or b) and grafted with NAG
Dependence of the grafting yield of NAG onto different PP treated
either under conditions a or b on the concentration
F. Poncin-Epaillard, N. Me´dard, J.-C. Soutif
if a 10–13% monomer solution is used. From a previous
study13) indicating that the radical’s density on a PP film
treated under conditions a is about 40 radicals/nm2, the
mean length of the grafted chains are estimated to 29
Grafting kinetics seem to be similar whatever the plasma
conditions are (Fig. 3). 40 h forgrafting are needed to reach
a plateau in the grafting yield. The higher the quantity of
surface amino groups, the higher is the grafting yield.
The DTPA fixation can not be well characterized with the
FTIR-ATR spectroscopy, probably due to a lack of sensitiv-
ity of this technique since here only one molecule is
attached per site of the reactive surface. The more sensitive
XPS analysis shows an evolution of the different atomic
ratio (Tab. 2) towards the theoretical values of DTPA (%C
= 52, %O = 33, %N = 15). As noticed before with NAG
grafting, more carbon atoms, and in a less degree oxygen
and nitrogen atoms, are incorporated. Here again, the mod-
ified layer is less important than the analyzed surface. On
the blank sample, which has been exposed to the grafting
and metal solutions, no copper was detected. So no metal
adsorption occurs onto these surfaces, which is an impor-
tant feature for the following study. The metal detection by
XPS or chelating analyses would correspond to a chelation
and not to an adsorption phenomenon.
This high resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 4 and Tab. 3)
of the grafted sample shows different sub-structures pos-
Dependence of the grafting yield of NAG onto PP treated under
conditions a on the concentration
Dependence of the grafting yield of NAG onto PP treated under condi-
Reactivity of surface groups attached on a plasma treated poly(propylene) film ...
sessing distinct chemical surroundings. The C1S peak pre-
sents 4 components corresponding to different DTPA
bonds as described in the literature14) after taking into
account a charge effect of 0.5 eV. The major sub-structure
(D, centered at 284.6 eV) is assigned to hydrocarbon
C1C, C1H of DTPA and PP. The other sub-structure at
286.1 eV (C) is identified as C1N bonds of amino
groups. Carbon atoms in structures like N1C2O appear
at 287.6 eV (B). The last sub-structure at 288.8 eV (A) is
assigned to O1C2O carbons of carboxylic groups.
The grafting yield of DTPA is calculated from an acid-
base titration of the fixed acid groups following the equa-
A dependence of grafting yield on the DTPA anhydride
concentration is shown in Fig. 5. A plateau appears around
6–8% of DTPA in the solution and could be explained by
an increase of the solution viscosity and a lower diffusion
of the DTPA anhydride to the reactive species.
The highest amino group concentration for a pure nitro-
gen plasma (about 2 NH2/nm2 in the plasma conditions
a)6) can be improved by changing the plasma chemistry,
for example by using an ammonia plasma or by adding
hydrogen to the nitrogen phase16). For a better control of
the plasma composition, we have chosen the second one.
With respect to the pre-chosen plasma conditions
(Pi = 60 W, t = 2 min), PP film is treated in different plas-
mas composed of nitrogen and hydrogen mixtures in differ-
ent proportions. The FTIR-ATR spectroscopy gives an evi-
dence of the functionalization. In comparison with the vir-
gin PP spectrum, several bands with weak intensity appear
around 4000–3000 cm–1 corresponding to msNH2 and
masNH2 vibration bands and around 2000–1500 cm–1 (cen-
tered at 1643 cm–1) corresponding to the dNHvibration.
The dependence of the surface amino group concentra-
tion titrated as described elsewhere4) onto the hydrogen
proportion in the plasma phase is illustrated in Fig. 6. The
surface amino groups density is increasing with the
hydrogen concentration until a critical concentration of
65 vol.-%, not too far from the stoichiometry ratio of
High resolution C1s XPS spectrum for treated PP under conditions a and
XPS analysis of PP film treated with nitrogen plasma
(conditions a), grafted with DTPA and chelated with Cu2+
Grafted and chelated with Cu2+ (a)
C1s peak fitting of XPS spectrum of PP films treated in
nitrogen plasma (conditions a) and grafted with DTPA
F. Poncin-Epaillard, N. Me´dard, J.-C. Soutif
NH2, then it decreases. This maximum corresponds to
around 6 amino sites per nm2 onto PP surface. The
decrease of the surface functionalization is probably asso-
ciated to a decrease of NH radicals in the plasma phase as
the nitrogen percentage is lowered. These proportions of
the gaseous mixture are used for the study of the fixation
and chelation of DTPA onto PP surface.
The characterization of the DTPA attachment onto
such an activated PP film with XPS and carboxylic and
amino groups titration shows an increase of the attach-
ment yield in a ratio of 3 compared to nitrogen plasma.
However, the reaction with DTPA anhydride on the sur-
face is probably incomplete due to a steric hindrance,
since the amino group density was increased by a factor
of 7. In spite of this low yield, the increase of the attached
DTPA onto the PP film allows an increase of the chelat-
Chelation study of the different PP films
This study is carried out onto three types of samples:
– PP film treated under plasma conditions a and
grafted with NAG (during 48 h with a 12 wt.-%
– PP films treated under conditions a, and grafted with
DTPA (at room temperature during 48 h with 8 wt.-
– PP film treated in N2/H2 (40/60) plasma under condi-
tions a and then grafted with DTPA (at room tempera-
ture during 48 h with 8 wt.-% monomer solution).
The chelation study is both run with mono or ternary
metallic solutions. The results of chelation with mono-
metallic solutions (composed of one metal ion in a con-
centration of salt around 2 N 10–4 mol/l) are given in
Tab. 4. Some values of the stoichiometry are quite sur-
Dependence of the grafting yield of DTPA onto PP treated under
conditions a on its concentration
Dependence of amino and acid groups onto PP on hydrogen con-
centration in the plasma phase
Reactivity of surface groups attached on a plasma treated poly(propylene) film ...
prising; other groups than acids (i.e. amide) could chelate
some metal. For the grafted films with DTPA and NAG, a
more pronounced chelation towards the weak acid Ag+ is
observed. From the HSAB17, 18) (Hard and Soft Acids and
Bases) theory predicting the formation of strong bonds
between either hard acids and bases, or soft acids and
bases, the behavior of the grafted DTPA is comparable to
The chelation yields calculated for the samples grafted
with DTPA are always lower than those grafted with
NAG whatever the surface amino groups density, because
of the length of the grafted macromolecular chains.
Results with ternary metal solution (composed of three
metal ions in a concentration of each salt equal to 2 N 10–4
mol N l–1 for the grafted NAG film and 2 N 10–5 mol N l–1 for
the DTPA grafted film) are given in Tab. 5. The DTPA
grafted film presents a high selectivity towards the chela-
tion of silver. The capacity of the different films determined
as the sum of the different chelated ions is around 4.3 N 10–9
mol N cm–2 and corresponds to a fairly high value since only
one tetrafunctional unit is fixed per amino group. The capa-
city of the NAG grafted film is much higher: 2.9 N 10–7
mol N cm–2than fortheDTPAgrafted film butthe selectivity
isweaker, even if it is still important.
In this study, an illustration of the use of cold plasma
treatment is given. A control of the surface properties is
possible, either by the incorporation of amino groups or
by the formation at radicals at the surface which allow in
both cases the attachment of an organic molecule or of a
monomer leading to chelating films.
All the grafted films present an important selectivity
towards silver ions and consequently towards soft metal
ions. The capacity of DTPA grafted films remains lower
than with polyNAG regardless the density of amino
groups attached onto the surface.
